mission.title: Install and Configure K3s (Lightweight Kubernetes)
mission.description: K3s is a lightweight, fully conformant Kubernetes distribution designed for resource-constrained environments. It is ideal for edge computing, IoT, and development scenarios where a full Kubernetes installation may be too heavy. Key trade-offs include a simplified architecture and reduced resource consumption at the cost of some advanced Kubernetes features.

steps.title: Install K3s Binary
steps.description:
This step installs the K3s binary on your server, which is the core component of K3s.

```bash

curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -

```

> This command fetches and installs the latest K3s version.

steps.title: Verify K3s Installation
steps.description:
Check that K3s is running correctly and the necessary services are up.

```bash

sudo k3s kubectl get nodes

```

> This command lists the nodes in your K3s cluster.

steps.title: Set Up Kubeconfig
steps.description:
Configure your kubeconfig file to interact with the K3s cluster using kubectl.

```bash

sudo k3s kubectl config view --raw > ~/.kube/config

```

> This command saves the K3s kubeconfig to your home directory.

steps.title: Deploy a Sample Application
steps.description:
Deploy a simple application to verify that your K3s cluster is functioning as expected.

```bash

kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx

kubectl expose deployment nginx --port=80 --type=NodePort

```

> This command deploys an Nginx server.
> This command exposes the Nginx deployment.

steps.title: Verify Application Deployment
steps.description:
Check that the application is running and accessible.

```bash

kubectl get services

```

> This command lists the services to find the NodePort.

uninstall.title: Uninstall K3s
uninstall.description:
This step removes K3s and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all workloads and data in the cluster.

```bash

sudo /usr/local/bin/k3s-uninstall.sh

```

> This command uninstalls K3s.

upgrade.title: Upgrade K3s
upgrade.description:
Backup your current configuration, perform the upgrade, and verify the installation.

```bash

kubectl get all --all-namespaces > k3s-backup.txt

curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -

sudo k3s kubectl get nodes

```

> Backup current resources.
> This command fetches and installs the latest K3s version.
> Verify that nodes are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'k3s is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** K3s service may not be started or is failing to start.

**Fix:**
Check the logs with 'sudo journalctl -u k3s' to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kubeconfig may not be set up correctly or K3s is not running.

**Fix:**
Ensure K3s is running and kubeconfig is correctly set with 'sudo k3s kubectl config view --raw > ~/.kube/config'.

troubleshooting.title: Pods are stuck in 'Pending' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient resources available in the cluster.

**Fix:**
Check node resources with 'kubectl describe nodes' and ensure there are enough resources allocated.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified container image may not be available or accessible.

**Fix:**
Verify the image name and check network connectivity to the image registry.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new CNI plugins, improved metrics server integration.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for certain legacy storage backends.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.30, ensure to check the compatibility of your existing workloads.

resolution.summary: You should see output similar to 'NAME                 STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION' when running 'kubectl get nodes'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure your environment meets the minimum resource requirements.
